Research Report: Recent Advances in Difference-in-Differences with Staggered Interventions

Topic: Staggered treatment timing, two-way fixed effects (TWFE), and modern robust estimators

tldr;

The canonical DiD design assumes a single treatment date. In practice, policies and interventions are often adopted at different times across units — a staggered adoption design. The traditional two-way fixed effects (TWFE) estimator, long the default, has been shown to produce a contaminated weighted average of underlying treatment effects when timing varies and effects are heterogeneous. A large literature (Goodman-Bacon 2021; Callaway & Sant’Anna 2021; Sun & Abraham 2021; de Chaisemartin & D’Haultfœuille 2020; Borusyak, Jaravel & Spiess 2024; Wooldridge 2021/2023) now provides robust alternatives that estimate cohort-time average treatment effects (ATTs) using only clean controls and aggregate them with transparent weights.

Bottom line: Abandon naive TWFE under staggered timing unless effects are provably homogeneous. Estimate cohort-time or event-study ATTs with csdid / did2s / did_imputation (or Wooldridge’s interaction-weighted framework) and report those instead of a single coefficient.

1. The Setting: Staggered Adoption

In staggered adoption, units become treated at different calendar times \(g \in \{2, \dots, T\}\). A unit \(i\) in cohort \(G_i = g\) is treated in all periods \(t \geq g\) and untreated in \(t < g\). This structure is pervasive: state laws adopted in different years, phased trade liberalization, platform feature rollouts.

The estimand of interest is the ATT, potentially varying by cohort \(g\) and by event time \(k = t - g\).

2. The TWFE Estimator and Why It Breaks

The workhorse specification:

\[Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \beta D_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}\]

where \(D_{it} = \mathbf{1}(t \geq G_i)\). This is intuitive and easy to estimate, but with staggered timing it is no longer a clean DiD.

Technical derivation: the Goodman–Bacon decomposition

Goodman-Bacon (2021) showed that the TWFE coefficient is a weighted average of all possible 2×2 DiD estimators comparing pairs of cohorts \((g, g')\):

\[\hat{\beta}_{TWFE} = \sum_{g \neq g'} w_{g,g'} \, \widehat{\text{DiD}}_{g,g'}\]

The weights \(w_{g,g'}\) are non-negative in total but individual comparisons can receive negative weight. The problematic terms compare an early-treated cohort \(g\) against a later-treated cohort \(g'\) that is used as a “control” in windows where \(g'\) is already treated. These are the forbidden comparisons (already-treated-as-control terms).

The three core failures:

  1. Forbidden comparisons — Already-treated units serve as controls for later-treated units; their outcomes are post-treatment and contaminated.
  2. Negative weights — Even when every underlying ATT is positive and constant, \(\hat{\beta}_{TWFE}\) can come out negative because forbidden 2×2s receive negative weights.
  3. Heterogeneity bias — If effects differ by cohort \(g\) or event time \(k = t-g\), the contamination is signed and biases \(\beta\) in an unspecified direction.

Important nuance: TWFE remains consistent under homogeneous effects (\(\tau_{gt} = \tau\) for all \(g,t\)), because every 2×2 — forbidden or not — equals \(\tau\), so the weighted average is still \(\tau\). The problem is entirely about effect heterogeneity interacting with timing.

3. Wooldridge’s Contributions and the TWFE Connection

Jeffrey Wooldridge has been central in clarifying exactly what TWFE estimates and how to repair it without abandoning the fixed-effects framework.

3.1 Unconditional vs. Conditional TWFE

Wooldridge emphasizes distinguishing the conditional TWFE (estimated at the individual level with \(D_{it}\)) from the unconditional formulation written in terms of group-time population averages:

\[\bar{Y}_{gt} = \alpha_g + \lambda_t + \tau_{gt}\,\mathbf{1}(t \geq g) + \bar{\varepsilon}_{gt}\]

For a balanced panel, the two are numerically identical, but the unconditional version makes the comparison structure transparent: the TWFE coefficient is revealed as a weighted average of group-time average treatment effects (GTATs) \(\tau_{gt}\), with weights given by cohort-size shares times pre/post period shares.

3.2 The Interaction-Weighted (IW) Estimator

Wooldridge’s proposed fix: estimate the GTAT \(\tau_{gt}\) for each \((g,t)\) using only clean controls (never-treated or not-yet-treated units), then aggregate with economically meaningful weights:

\[\widehat{\text{ATT}}^{IW} = \frac{\sum_{g} \sum_{t \geq g} N_g \, \pi_{gt} \, \hat{\tau}_{gt}}{\sum_{g} \sum_{t \geq g} N_g \, \pi_{gt}}\]

where \(N_g\) is the size of cohort \(g\) and \(\pi_{gt}\) is the post-treatment period weight for that cohort-time cell. The IW weights are \(N_g \times (\text{share of post periods for cohort }g)\), so larger cohorts and longer post-treatment windows dominate the aggregate ATT — exactly the policy-relevant weighting. This yields a properly weighted overall ATT robust to heterogeneous effects that excludes forbidden comparisons by construction.

3.3 Efficient Estimation and Event Studies

In more recent work (2023–2024), Wooldridge develops efficient GMM estimators that exploit the known treatment-timing structure to recover precision while preserving robustness. He also shows how to specify a clean event-study (relative-time) model directly at the group-time level, permitting valid pre-trend tests without the contamination that plagues the naive TWFE event study:

\[Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \sum_{k \neq -1} \delta_k \, \mathbf{1}(t - G_i = k) + \varepsilon_{it}\]

where \(k = -1\) is the omitted reference period and the counterfactual is built only from not-yet/never-treated units.

Details

Wooldridge’s IW estimator is conceptually close to Callaway–Sant’Anna (2021): both estimate cohort-time ATTs and aggregate with transparent weights. Differences lie in (i) the exact weighting scheme and (ii) Wooldridge’s emphasis on the unconditional FE representation and later efficient GMM extensions. Sun–Abraham (2021) instead uses binned relative-time indicators plus a reweighting that zeroes out forbidden comparisons. de Chaisemartin–D’Haultfœuille (2020) use only “clean” switch-out 2×2s. Borusyak–Jaravel–Spiess (2024) impute counterfactuals from the TWFE residual structure. All share the same diagnosis of the TWFE failure.

4. The Broader Toolkit (Comparison)

Estimator Core Strategy Uses Forbidden Comparisons? Robust to Hetero. Effects?
Naive TWFE \(\alpha_i + \lambda_t + \beta D_{it}\) Yes No
Callaway–Sant’Anna (2021) Cohort-time ATT \(\to\) aggregate No Yes
Sun–Abraham (2021) Binned event-study + reweight No Yes
de Chaisemartin–D’Haultfœuille (2020) Clean “switch-out” 2×2s No Yes
Borusyak–Jaravel–Spiess (2024) Impute missing counterfactuals No (implicitly) Yes
Wooldridge (IW / efficient) Unconditional GTAT + weighting No Yes

5. Core Estimation Issues — Summary

  1. Negative weighting / contamination — TWFE mixes treated-vs-treated comparisons; the coefficient can be a weighted average with negative weights.
  2. Heterogeneous treatment effects — Variation in ATT across cohorts (\(g\)) or event time (\(k = t-g\)) induces bias under TWFE; the sign is generally unknown.
  3. Dynamic / event-study misspecification — Naive TWFE event studies attribute pre-trends and post-effects inconsistently when timing varies.
  4. Choice of control group — Must use never-treated or not-yet-treated units; already-treated units are invalid controls.
  5. Parallel trends assumption — Required conditionally on the chosen control set; only pre-trends are testable, not post-treatment counterfactuals.
  6. Attrition / unbalanced panels — Staggered designs need care in weighting; Wooldridge’s unconditional approach helps by working at the group-time level.
  7. Efficiency vs. robustness trade-off — Simple aggregation is robust but can lose precision; efficient GMM recovers precision using timing structure.

6. Software Recommendations & Implementation

6.1 Recommended Workflow

  1. Run bacondecomp (or twowayfeweights) first. If the “timing-of-treatment” weight is small, naive TWFE may be acceptable; if large, abandon it.
  2. Estimate at least two of: Callaway–Sant’Anna (csdid / did), Sun–Abraham (eventstudyinteract / did2s / fixest), and Borusyak et al. (did_imputation). Convergence across methods builds confidence.
  3. Report cohort-time or event-study ATTs, not a single number, whenever effects are likely heterogeneous.

Details

R

  • In did::att_gt, never-treated units must be coded as first_treat = 0 (or a value larger than any time); Inf also works.
  • fixest::feols is dramatically faster than plm/lm for large panels; use vcov = ~id for clustered SEs.
  • The Sun–Abraham fixest approach and the dedicated did2s give very similar event-study coefficients but differ in how they handle never-treated vs. not-yet-treated reference cohorts.
  • Always plot the pre-treatment coefficients (e.g., ggdid(agg_event)) to eyeball parallel trends.

Stata

  • csdid expects gvar() to be 0 for never-treated (not missing). If your never-treated are coded ., recode: replace first_treat = 0 if missing(first_treat).
  • eventstudyinteract requires a control_cohort variable distinguishing never-treated from eventually-treated units.
  • reghdfe is the workhorse for fast high-dimensional FE; pair with vce(cluster id) for inference.
  • After csdid, use estat event, window(-5 5) to restrict the plot window.
  • did_imputation and did2s both exploit the “clean” not-yet/never-treated counterfactual and are good robustness companions to csdid.

7. References

Paper Venue Link
Goodman-Bacon, A. (2021) — “Difference-in-Differences with Variation in Treatment Timing” Journal of Econometrics, 225(2): 254–277 doi:10.1016/j.jeconom.2021.03.014
Callaway, B. & Sant’Anna, P. (2021) — “Difference-in-Differences with Multiple Time Periods” Journal of Econometrics, 225(2): 200–230 doi:10.1016/j.jeconom.2021.03.015
Sun, L. & Abraham, S. (2021) — “Estimating Dynamic Treatment Effects in Event Studies with Heterogeneous Treatment Effects” Journal of Econometrics, 225(2): 175–199 doi:10.1016/j.jeconom.2021.03.010
de Chaisemartin, C. & D’Haultfœuille, X. (2020) — “Two-Way Fixed Effects Estimators with Heterogeneous Treatment Effects” American Economic Review, 110(9): 2964–2996 doi:10.1257/aer.20181169
Borusyak, K., Jaravel, X. & Spiess, J. (2024) — “Revisiting Event Study Designs” (corrected: JOE, not JEEA) Journal of Econometrics, 235(2): 425–455 doi:10.1016/j.jeconom.2023.03.008
Gardner, J. (2022) — “Two-Stage Differences in Differences” arXiv working paper arXiv:2207.05943
Wooldridge, J. M. (2021/2023) — “Two-Way Fixed Effects, the Event Study, and the Pretrends Test” Working paper (Michigan State) msu.edu/~ecwool · related SSRN: abstract=4189389

Note on Wooldridge’s link: The TWFE/event-study working paper is circulated from his faculty page rather than a DOI’d journal (as of 2024–2026 it remains a widely-cited WP). The SSRN link is to his closely related 2022 paper on nonlinear DiD with panel data.

Software documentation links

  • did (R): https://bcallaway11.github.io/did/
  • fixest (R): https://lrberge.github.io/fixest/
  • did2s (R): https://github.com/kylebutts/did2s
  • didimputation (R): https://github.com/kylebutts/didimputation
  • csdid (Stata): help csdid after ssc install csdid (author: Fernando Rios-Avila)
  • eventstudyinteract (Stata): help eventstudyinteract (Sun & Abraham)

Difference in Difference

  • A nice tracker of DiD packages for R

  • A nice exploration of the two-way Mundlak model by Badi Baltagi
